title: How to Stop Apologizing for Wanting More Money
body:
Stop Apologizing for Wanting More Money. Seriously.

You feel it, don't you? That little flicker of shame, the whisper of guilt, whenever the topic of wanting more money comes up. You might even hear your own voice, internally or externally, downplaying your desires, justifying your ambition, or outright apologizing for it. It's a pervasive, insidious script we've all been handed, and it's time we ripped it up. Because here's the thing: apologizing for wanting more money isn't humility, it's self-sabotage. It's a deep-seated poverty consciousness masquerading as virtue, and it's keeping you from your abundance. You feel that?

The Shame Spiral of Scarcity

Let's get real. The idea that wanting money is somehow "bad" or "greedy" is a story. A powerful, sticky, damaging story. It's woven into the fabric of our culture, whispered in church pews, shouted from social media soapboxes. You're told that true spirituality means detachment, that real generosity comes from having little, that striving for more means you're materialistic and shallow. And you've bought into it, haven't you? Even a little bit. Know what I mean?

Brené Brown's work on shame is incredibly relevant here. Shame thrives in secrecy and judgment. When you feel ashamed for wanting financial security, for desiring comfort, for aspiring to a life where money isn't a constant source of stress, you isolate yourself. You stop talking about it. You certainly stop asking for it. This isn't just about external judgment; it's about the internal critic that's internalized these societal messages. That voice tells you you're not good enough to have it, or that if you do get it, you'll be "one of them" - the rich, the uncaring, the disconnected. It's a defense mechanism, a way to protect yourself from perceived judgment, but it ultimately keeps you small and trapped in a cycle of lack.

The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. You could be making six figures, and if you're still apologizing for wanting more, for wanting to save more, invest more, give more, you're still operating from a place of scarcity. It's not about the number in your bank account; it's about the narrative in your head. And that narrative is costing you more than just money. It's costing you peace, freedom, and the ability to fully express your unique gifts in the world.

Unpacking the "Good Person" Myth

Honestly, let's dissect this "good person" myth for a second. Who decided that a "good person" is someone who struggles financially? Who said that true virtue lies in self-sacrifice to the point of personal deprivation? These are not universal truths; they're cultural constructs, often perpetuated by those who benefit from your struggle. Think about it. If you're constantly worried about money, you're less likely to challenge the status quo, less likely to take risks, less likely to pursue your biggest dreams. You're easier to control.

Kate Northrup, in her book The Art of Money (paid link), talks about how our relationship with money is often a reflection of our relationship with ourselves. If you're constantly downplaying your desires for money, you're also likely downplaying your worth, your talent, your capacity for impact. You're essentially saying, "I'm not worthy of more." And the universe, being an excellent listener, tends to agree. It's not magic; it's psychology. Your subconscious beliefs shape your actions, and your actions shape your reality.

What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? Think about it. Money doesn't judge your past, it doesn't care about your intentions, it simply reflects your beliefs and actions. If you believe you don't deserve it, you'll find ways to push it away. If you believe it's evil, you'll avoid it. If you believe it's scarce, you'll hoard it or fear its loss. There's no hiding from your money story.

The Trauma of Scarcity

Look, for many of us, the roots of this money shame go deep. They're not just cultural; they're personal, familial, even generational. You might have grown up in a household where money was a constant source of stress, arguments, or secrecy. You might have witnessed parents or caregivers struggling, making sacrifices, or feeling bitter about their financial situation. These experiences leave imprints. They become part of your nervous system's programming. As Bessel van der Kolk powerfully articulates in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), trauma - and financial trauma is absolutely a thing - isn't just a memory in your mind; it's stored in your body. It affects your fight, flight, or freeze response when it comes to money. That anxiety you feel when checking your bank balance? That's not just "being responsible." That's your body's old wiring kicking in.

Healing this isn't about affirmations alone. It's about acknowledging the pain, the fear, the stories you've inherited. It's about recognizing that your past financial experiences shaped your present reactions, but they don't have to dictate your future. This isn't about blaming your parents or your circumstances; it's about understanding the origins of your patterns so you can consciously choose to create new ones. It's about radical acceptance of your financial reality, and then radical action to change it. Tara Brach's Radical Acceptance (paid link) is a powerful guide for this kind of deep, compassionate self-inquiry.

When you apologize for wanting more money, you're essentially apologizing for wanting safety, for wanting options, for wanting to contribute more fully to the world. You're apologizing for a fundamental human drive towards growth and expansion. Does that land?

Reclaiming Your Desire: It's Not About Greed

Let's be clear: wanting more money doesn't automatically make you greedy. Greed is an attachment to accumulation for its own sake, often at the expense of others. Wanting more money, for most people, comes from a place of wanting security, freedom, impact, and joy. It's wanting to pay off debt, to provide for your family, to travel, to support causes you believe in, to have the time and resources to pursue your passions, to not be constantly stressed about bills. These are not inherently selfish desires. In fact, many of them are deeply altruistic.

Sam Harris, in his discussions on ethics and well-being, often points out that our actions have consequences. If your financial struggle limits your ability to be present for your loved ones, to contribute your unique talents to the world, or to simply experience joy, then that struggle has negative consequences. Conversely, financial well-being can create more positive consequences - not just for you, but for your community and the world at large. A person who is financially secure is often a more generous person, a more creative person, a more engaged person.

The apology for wanting more money often stems from a fear of being perceived as "bad" or "selfish." But what if the opposite is true? What if your inability to ask for and receive more money is actually limiting your capacity to do good in the world? What if your financial struggle is actually creating more suffering, for you and for those around you, because you're constantly operating from a place of fear and lack?

Practical Steps to Un-Apologize

So, how do you stop this insidious apology? It's a journey, not a destination, but here are some concrete steps:

  • Acknowledge the desire: Stop pretending you don't want more money. Say it out loud. "I want more money." Feel the discomfort. Let's sit with the discomfort of that number for a moment. What thoughts come up? What sensations in your body? Don't judge them, just observe.
  • Identify the "why": Get specific about why you want more money. Is it for security? Freedom? Contribution? A specific experience? The more clear you are on your positive motivations, the easier it is to release the shame.
  • Challenge the narrative: When that voice of shame or guilt pops up, question it. "Who told me this? Is it actually true? What would happen if I believed the opposite?"
  • Practice asking for what you're worth: This is where the rubber meets the road. In your job, in your business, in your relationships - practice setting boundaries around your financial value. Negotiate. Ask for raises. Charge what you're worth. It will feel uncomfortable at first. That's good. It means you're growing.
  • Educate yourself: Money anxiety often comes from a lack of understanding. Learn about personal finance, investing, budgeting. Bari Tessler's work in The Art of Money (paid link) is a fantastic starting point for building a conscious relationship with your finances. Knowledge is power, and it dispels fear.
  • Surround yourself with abundance-minded people: The people you spend time with profoundly influence your beliefs. Seek out those who celebrate financial success, who talk about money openly and positively, and who inspire you to grow.
  • Practice gratitude for what you have: This isn't about settling. It's about shifting your energetic vibration from lack to abundance. When you genuinely appreciate what you already have, you open yourself up to receiving more. It's not either/or; it's both/and.
  • Give generously: This might sound counterintuitive when you're focusing on wanting more, but giving is a powerful way to reinforce an abundance mindset. It tells your subconscious, "I have enough, and more than enough, to share." It shifts you from a scarcity mentality to a flow mentality.
  • Heal your money trauma: If you find yourself consistently stuck, despite your best efforts, it might be time to explore deeper. Consider working with a therapist or coach who specializes in financial psychology or trauma. There's no shame in seeking support for something that's so deeply wired.

This isn't about becoming a ruthless capitalist. It's about becoming a conscious human being who understands that money is a tool. A powerful tool. And like any tool, its impact depends on the hands that wield it. When you apologize for wanting more money, you're essentially saying you're not capable of wielding that tool responsibly, ethically, or for the greater good. And that's just not true.

FAQ

Q: Isn't wanting more money inherently selfish?

A: Not necessarily. While it's possible to want money for purely selfish reasons, for most people, the desire for more money stems from wanting security, freedom, to provide for loved ones, and to make a greater impact. It's about having options and reducing stress, which often allows you to be more generous and present for others.

Q: What if I feel guilty when I actually get more money?

A: That's a common experience, often rooted in the shame and scarcity narratives we discussed. It's crucial to acknowledge this guilt without judgment. Understand its origins - perhaps from family messages about money being "evil" or "corrupting." Then, consciously reframe your relationship with this new abundance. Focus on the positive impact you can create with it, the freedom it offers, and the security it provides. Giving a portion away can also help alleviate guilt by reinforcing your capacity for generosity.

Q: How can I change my mindset if I've always struggled with money?

A: Changing a deeply ingrained mindset takes consistent effort and compassion. Start by observing your thoughts and feelings about money without judgment. Educate yourself about personal finance to build confidence. Practice gratitude for what you have. Visualize your desired financial future and truly feel what it would be like. Surround yourself with people who have a healthy relationship with money. And be patient with yourself - this isn't an overnight fix. Small, consistent steps lead to significant shifts over time.

Your desire for more money is not a flaw; it's a signal. A signal that you're ready for more, that you have more to give, that you're capable of more. Stop apologizing for it. Embrace it. And take one small, uncomfortable step today towards claiming the abundance that's waiting for you.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Stop apologizing for wanting more money. Understand why you feel guilty and learn to embrace your ambition. Discover strategies to confidently pursue financial
author: Kalesh
